availability.p: Published under a Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

editorialDecl.p: Encoding of this text has followed the recommendations of the LombardPress 1.0.0 guidelines for a diplomatic edition.

change.p: Created file for the first time.

div.head: Chapter 10 [Bnf 12926 Transcription]
div.p: Chapter 10

¶ Eighth: that the Church cannot punish idolaters is proved from Augustine on Psalm 145, where, expounding the verse, "The Lord renders judgment for all who suffer injustice," he says: concerning those who are not under its own jurisdiction (that is, of the Church), upon whom discipline cannot be exercised — although I find those very words nowhere in that place. But because Augustine is cited by Gratian, Causa 23, q. 4, § "To these, therefore, a reply is made," under the canon Cum in lege where Gratian subjoins the following: there are certain things which are to be corrected by wholesome admonition alone, not to be punished by bodily chastisements; and their retribution is to be reserved to divine judgment alone, when we cannot exercise discipline upon offenders — either because they are not subject to our jurisdiction, or because, even if their crimes are known to us, they cannot be proved by clear evidence. Concerning those who are not subject to our jurisdiction, the Apostle says in the First Letter to the Corinthians: For what is it to me to judge those who are outside? The Lord will judge these. And so forth.
Thus Gratian.

Therefore, if they are not subject to our jurisdiction, we cannot punish them in any way. The same is taught by Augustine in Homily 50 on the Worthlessness and Necessity of Penance, col. 6. And from there Gratian drew what he reports in c. Multi, C. 2, q. 1. More explicitly, Augustine in the book On Baptism is read in c. Non ne, C. 1, q. 1, where he says: Were not the Sodomites Gentiles, that is, pagans, and worse therefore than the Jews, to whom the Lord says: "It shall be more tolerable for Sodom on the day of judgment than for you," and to whom the prophet says: "You have justified Sodom," that is, by comparison with you Sodom has been made just? And further: For even the Gentiles themselves, if they were able to hold anything divine and right in their teachings, the holy men did not disapprove. Although those Gentiles, on account of their superstitions and idolatry, their pride and their other corrupt morals, were to be abhorred; and unless corrected, were to be punished by divine judgment. Thus Augustine.
Do you see that Augustine holds that the punishment of crimes committed by pagans or idolaters is reserved to divine judgment? Therefore, such punishment does not belong to the Church.

¶ Ninth: this truth is proved from the argument upon which all the other arguments rest. Saint Thomas sets it forth in Book IV of the Sentences, distinction 17, q. 3, art. 1, question 5, in the body of the article. The argument is this. The Pope, the vicar of Christ, and the other ministers of the Church were established in the Church after the Church was divinely instituted. Hence it is certain that the building up, or institution, of the Church preceded and is presupposed by the ministries and acts of the Church's ministers; just as the works of creation preceded and are presupposed by the works of nature. Now this institution of the Church consists of faith and the sacraments. The sacraments necessarily presuppose that there must be certain persons capable and fitted to receive the sacraments — namely, those made capable through faith. But pagans and unbelievers, since they are entirely outside the Church and are not members of the Church because they lack faith, are incapable of the sacraments; and thus the institution of the Church does not in any way pertain to them. From this it follows that the works or acts of the Church's ministers do not extend to them, just as the operation of nature does not extend to things not yet created, or to things that do not yet exist in the natural order.

But jurisdiction and the operations or judicial acts by which offenses are punished belong to the ministers of the Church by reason of faith and the sacraments, which must necessarily be presupposed to the acts of the ministers — just as the work of creation must be presupposed in order for nature to be able to act. Therefore, the ministers of the Church cannot, as a rule, exercise jurisdiction over pagans or unbelievers, since they are not capable subjects of such operations of the Church. From which it is inferred that if, by an impossible supposition, the Apostles had converted no one to the faith, they would have been entirely unable to exercise the jurisdiction granted to them by Christ, since, with no Church existing among them,
there would have been lacking a subject or matter upon which to exercise the acts of their jurisdiction.
Therefore, the punishment of idolatry, or of any sin whatsoever of pagans or unbelievers, does not belong to the Church.

¶ Tenth: in the conversion of unbelievers who have never received the faith, to whom the faith has not been preached, and who have not resisted hearing the faith — with respect to whom this will be called the first preaching of the faith — one must not begin with the punishment of sins, but with the offer of clemency and forgiveness and with exhortation to repentance. This is proved because, if we begin by punishing their crimes, we overturn the express commandment and institution of Christ, and the form of preaching prescribed to preachers. For Christ, sending the Apostles to preach the Gospel to every creature, addressed them in the words recorded by Luke in the final chapter: "Thus it was written, and thus it was necessary for Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead on the third day, and that repentance and forgiveness of sins should be preached in his name to all nations, beginning from Jerusalem; and you are witnesses of these things" — namely, "those things which are to be preached," according to the interlinear gloss. And on the word "all," the same interlinear gloss says: "Not in one nation only, that of the Jews, nor in any one region of the earth." Thus the gloss.

And on the phrase "beginning from Jerusalem" and so forth, the Glossa Ordinaria says: It was fitting that the ministers who were to preach repentance and forgiveness to all nations should begin from Jerusalem — not only because to them were entrusted the oracles of God, and because theirs is the adoption as children, and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law; but also so that the nations, entangled in various errors and crimes, might be drawn to the hope of pardon by this supreme act of divine mercy: that pardon was granted even to those who had crucified the Son of God. Thus the gloss.

¶ From these words it is proved, first, that Christ commanded the Apostles to preach the Gospel by freely offering and promising forgiveness of sins. Likewise, that they should exhort those people to repentance. That this was indeed a command is proved from those words, "thus it was written," that is, thus it was prophesied; therefore it was necessary "for Christ to suffer." Given, therefore, the divine pre-ordination and the necessity of the end — which was to free and redeem the human race, concerning which Saint Thomas, Part III, q. 46, a. 1 and in Book III of the Sentences, distinction 20, a. 1, question 3 — by the same manner and by the same necessity it was necessary to preach repentance and forgiveness of sins to all nations.

For just as it was necessary for Christ to suffer and to rise from the dead for the redemption of the human race — both because it was thus prophesied, and because it had been pre-ordained by the Lord on account of the necessity of the end — so too it had been ordained by God and foretold by the prophets many years beforehand, and it was also necessary for the fulfillment of the counsel and will of God, "who wills all people to be saved," especially the predestined, that the evangelical truth should be announced — beginning with the exhortation of people to repentance and promising them, on behalf of God and Jesus Christ, pardon and forgiveness of all their sins.

This was so necessary that, unless the Gospel had been announced in this manner to those who at that time were ignorant of the truth, the predestined would certainly not have attained salvation; and thus the fruit of Christ's passion would have been rendered void and null — in accordance with that verse of the Psalmist in Psalm 29: "What profit is there in my blood, if I descend into corruption?"

But this — namely, that the predestined should fail to attain salvation — was impossible. Therefore, it was entirely and necessarily required that the Apostles should evangelize in accordance with the form, commandment, and institution given to them by Christ.

This consequence is clear. For when God predestined those who would attain salvation, he also predestined the means by which salvation was to come to them. This is taught by Saint Thomas, Part I, q. 28, a. 8. Now the first and foremost means was the passion of the Son of God; and the second and foremost after the first, presupposing divine grace, was the announcement of the Gospel in accordance with the commandment, form, and institution of Christ — in keeping with that verse of the
Apostle in Romans 10: "How will they call upon him in whom they have not believed? Or how will they believe in him whom they have not heard? How will they hear without a preacher?" Therefore, the preaching of the Gospel was a necessary means for the salvation of those whom God predestined; and consequently, the Apostles were entirely and necessarily obliged to proclaim the law of Christ in that manner.

Now the manner of evangelizing, in accordance with the institution and commandment of Christ, is to exhort people to repentance while offering them forgiveness of sins — as is clear from the words of Luke cited above, and from many other passages of the Gospel and the prophets. In confirmation of this, when Christ began to disseminate his heavenly teaching, he addressed people in these words: "Do penance, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand," Matthew 4. That word of Luke, it is necessary (oportet), therefore implies a commandment and introduces necessity — as is also proved from those words of John 3: "You must be born again."

This word it is necessary (oportet) entirely introduces necessity. For it is necessary for people to be reborn through the water of baptism in order to be saved — in accordance with that saying: "Unless one is born again of water and the Holy Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God," John 3. So also in John 10, the word it is necessary (oportet) introduces necessity: "I have other sheep that are not of this fold; and I must bring them also." These sheep are pagans, who are to be brought to the sheepfold of Christ by the preaching of evangelical truth. Therefore, that verse of Luke, "thus it was written" and so forth, and the word it is necessary (oportet), which introduces necessity, signifies that preachers of the Gospel are necessarily obliged to exhort people to repentance, offering them forgiveness of sins.

For just as there the word it is necessary (oportet), referred to the passion and resurrection of Christ, signifies that these were necessary for the salvation of the predestined, so too it signifies that the Gospel must be preached with exhortation to repentance and with the offer of forgiveness of sins. No announcer of the Gospel, therefore, can begin with the punishment of the crimes of pagans — especially of those to whom the faith has not yet been preached.

From these words of Augustine our position is abundantly confirmed — namely, that the Gospel must be announced to pagans with exhortation to repentance and with the offer of forgiveness of their sins. Furthermore, that word of Christ, it is necessary (oportet), introduces necessity — as the canonists teach in c. Nam concupiscentiam, de constitutionibus and in c. 1 de libelli oblatione and in c. 1 de rescriptis, Book VI, and in other parts; and it is also held by the civilians in l. 1 on the word oportet, ff. de iustitia et iure and so forth.

The rationale behind all of this is given by Augustine in Against Petilianus and is read in C. 23, q. 5, c. Ad fidem; he says: but the punishing discipline of evil living is preposterous — that is, disordered — unless the hard work of teaching good living precedes it; that is, unless the offender is first instructed in what is good, or unless the one who corrects first offers an example of good life, as the gloss explains.

You see that Augustine teaches that holy doctrine and the example of upright living must precede the punishment of a sinner. This holds all the more for sinners who sin in ignorance — such as those pagans who have never heard the words of Christ, concerning whom our present discourse has been established. Otherwise, punishment and discipline is preposterous and disordered, and devised by the spirit of the devil.

¶ Second, it is to be noted from those words of our Savior — namely, "thus it was written" and so forth — that the form in which he commanded his Gospel to be announced must be observed for whatever nations of the world are evangelized, indiscriminately, whether they are pagans or weighed down by the most atrocious sins; for the words are general. For Christ says: "to all nations," establishing no difference between one nation and another. Furthermore, we read in Acts 13: "Behold, we turn to the Gentiles." But it is a maxim in human law that where the law has made no distinction, neither should we make one. Since, therefore, the words of Christ are general, they must be observed wherever the Gospel is preached — by announcing the truth in accordance with the institution, example, and commandment of Christ and his Apostles.
